Well, this game is like you're the batter and they're throwing balls to you, but you can wait as long as you want. You don't have to take the next pitch. You don't have to take the next deal. It's about getting the right client and negotiating the right deal. And just know that once you get into negotiations, anything's possible. You can define it any way you want. You can define this business any way you want. You can create the business that you want. So every deal is a deal and everything is negotiable. Okay, so there's three and one of the... I'm gonna go over the three main types, the three primary ways of doing this. And what I want you to know is that I've done all three of these and I've done all three of these more than once. So this is all from hard fought experience, made a lot of money. Some of the deals have gone really well. Other deals have gone okay and some deals fell apart. So I've got a lot of experience to share with you. So the first one is the simplest and this is just where you're either consulting or you're coaching. ## [03:01 - 06:03]

basically unhierable at this point just because the reality is my business has grown so much that I can't afford to take days and work out of the people's business. But they paid me to come out, spend a day together and we talked about their launch and what the offer would look like and what the pre-launch would look like and how they would rally partners. So this type of deal is very, very simple. It's just X number of dollars for X amount of time and it could be a one-shot deal. That was a one-shot deal. One day we were in and out super clean. I don't have to worry about anything or it could be a monthly deal or it could be a weekly, it could be like a series of calls. And just because I got paid $10,000 for a day doesn't mean you have to. You could do it with a day for a lot less or you can do it for a lot more. Or it could be an hour or it could be a half a day. It could be a Skype session. It could be, you know, four calls. It could be anything you want at any dollar amount you want. But the reality is at the end of the day you're just coaching them. You're saying, yeah, did you think about this? Did you think about that? You might want to do this. You might want to do that. That plan looks too aggressive. The time and list looks a little crazy. Yeah, that script looks fantastic. You might want to add in these bullet points. I think the offer's a little weak here. You might be able to get these partners there. Anything like that. I mean, it's just coaching. It's consulting. It's very simple. This is by far the simplest deal to do. Now, the next type of deal to do is, and again, this is one I've done multiple times. And this is where you actually partner with someone. This is where someone typically might have a domain of knowledge. They're like the expert. They're the talent, so to speak. And then you're providing the marketing. Now, this is actually how I got started in this business. I had someone who had been a mentor for me in the financial markets. And we decided to start publishing in the financial markets about how to trade the financial markets. And we did a 50-50 deal. And the way it worked is he provided the content and I did everything else in the business, which was customer service, all the tech, all the actual publishing, all the marketing, all the website building, all the systems, all the accounting, everything that a business takes, an online business takes, which is a lot. And in the end, the content piece, which seemed like a really big deal at the beginning, then ends up being a pretty small piece. And so in this case, the 50-50, at least in my mind, worked out really well for my partner because he was just spending, you know, his half hour a day to write up the content each day. And I was spending, you know, eight or 10 hours a day to build the business. But that's the deal we did. Now, an interesting thing here is

## [06:03 - 09:05]

that after about eight years in the business, my partner basically stole the business. He took the business and he created a competing website. After watching me do all the work for eight years, he thought, oh, Jeff, he's getting half the money. It's not really worth it. And plus, I know all his tricks by now, so I can do my own marketing. So I tell you that just so that you know that that's not the first time this type of deal is broken down that way, where the content producer really started to think that they were the star, you know, they're the star of the show. It's my show. I can take it and go do what I want with it. That has happened to more than just myself. It's happened to, I know, several people's happened to. So that is a tendency for the talent, so to speak, to think that they are the business. So it's something to watch out for. The reality is this deal is actually better works as 7030, 70 for the marketing and the business and 30% for the talent. And if you look at the what talents paid in other areas, say like in Broadway or Hollywood or or in the athletics, I don't know the numbers, but I don't really think the talent tends to get 50%. I think it the talent tends to get less than 50%. The reality is the talent is replaceable. The business is not replaceable. The marketing is not replaceable. The talent is. If you look at someone like just to show how old I am, Johnny Carson used to do late night TV. I don't watch enough TV. I can remember what his show is called. It was a late night talk show, right? And he was on for 30 years. I went to Wikipedia said from 62 to 92 and he was this icon and he seemed irreplaceable. He was this amazing talent, but then he retired and they replaced him with Jay Leno. It was like no problem. Jay just went on like just no problem and then Jay was replaced by Conan O'Brien and the talent is replaceable. And so when you negotiate this deal, if you're bringing real marketing experience, if you're bringing real capability here, if you're building business building experience, consider that 50-50 is not that the way you should do that deal. It should be 70-30 and that might be hard for the talent to hear quote unquote talent to hear, but that's the reality is that in pretty much in any type of show business and in any type of business where there's a talent, talent is replaceable. The business is not replaceable. So I just put that out there. I have done deals multiple times 50-50. Many of them worked out really well. That one worked out well for eight years and then it went a little sideways. But this is a way to build a real business where you have real skin in the

## [09:05 - 12:07]

game. Now the third model is what we call the PLM model, the Product Launch Manager model. And I'm going to be going deep on this in another video, but I just want to mention this is like we were actually running the launch for someone. And when you're doing this just in terms of the deal, the thing that you hit, so first of all picking the right horse is super incredibly important here. So you'll be putting a lot of work in. And like I said, the one time I did it, I put a lot of work in and the product never launched. The person never brought brought me a finished product, could never get it done. And you know the reality is I've got projects I've never completed. We all probably have projects we haven't completed. You have to make sure you're working with someone that's going to complete the product. You have to get the right horse. And then this is absolutely critical. You have to get paid up front. So the way I would typically structure this deal is I would get paid X number of dollars up front. That's like just to sign the deal, to get on my calendar, to get on my schedule. We sign and I was back in the day, this is a decade ago, I was typically getting paid or 10 to 15 thousand dollars just to get in the calendar. And then I would get paid a percentage of the launch. And the way we structured it was we looked at what a good launch would be and it was 10% of the gross for the launch. And that was pre refunds. It was 10% of the gross of the launch. And then we had and then if we exceeded what like was just a good launch and it gradually went up to 15%. So maybe it was like the first million dollars was 10%. And then after million dollars it was 11%. And after 1.2 million dollars it was 12%. And after 1.5 it was 13%. And after 2 million it was 15%. And you want to negotiate whether those bumps are retroactive for the whole amount or just on a stair step. Remember every deal steals all negotiable but you have to define the stuff. Part of doing this deal is it's having to be crystal clear both sides of what you're paying what they're paying for what they're going to get what you're going to do how the dollars how the numbers are going to get calculated. It doesn't have to be some fancy legalese. I'm a fan of actually having something that's very readable. You know having a one pager that's super readable that regular humans you don't need lawyers to be able to understand what it is. But you have to do this you have to get the upfront money. Because at the end of the day you're going to put a lot of time and effort into this and you have to carve out a place on your schedule. You can say this August and September I'm going to work on this launch and if you're doing that if you're going to sign this deal and you're going to block off your calendar you need to be compensated for that. And

## [12:07 - 15:13]

that money is not refundable. If they don't end up ever launching a product you keep the money. That deal I made where we you know where it never shipped where we never actually launched. I didn't get any upfront money on that one. I screwed up. This is how I learned this lesson. So you need some upfront money. The other thing you need is a retainer. So say they want to launch in September or October that means September or October that means you're going to be working on in July and August. Maybe you're signing the deal in June. Okay so you signed the deal in June they gave you X number of dollars. And then for each month they have to pay you X number of dollars. So maybe it's you know back then for me upfront was $10,000 right but then each month is $500. So $10,000 upfront plus $500 a month and then if the launch gets delayed an extra three months fine but they keep on paying you that $500. This is basically your compensation for having this this big project in your head and in the offing that you have to have on your calendar. It's blocked off that chunk of time in your calendar. So upfront plus retainer plus percentage. Now every deal is a deal. You can make that upfront higher or lower and the percentage higher or lower and the retainer higher. All of those amounts are going to be negotiable. And don't go on. If you're just brand new to this and you haven't ever done one don't be thinking that Jeff got $10,000 so I have to charge them. If $5,000 makes sense to you do it that way. If $1,000 makes sense for you do it that way. But you have to work with someone that's willing to put skin in the game and pay you upfront because you have skin in the game. You have blocked off this area on your calendar. You're going to be putting a lot of work before that launch. You know it's going to be a couple months of work and then that launch happens and that's probably not going to be another 30 days until you get paid. It's going to be a long time with you not getting paid so you need to be paid upfront. You need to be paid that retainer. Do not be flexible on these at all. And oh by the way when you're doing that deal negotiate in exactly when you get paid. How many days after the launch does that percentage come in? So those are the three main ways to do this. One is very simple but least lucrative but super simple as the coaching consulting. The second is partnering and that's a big deal. It's a long-term commitment. I mean I did that for my first eight years in this business. It can be great. It can be fantastic but there are a few things to watch out for. And then there's that PLM model. And one final thing about the PLM, the Product Launch Manager model. One final piece about that is that there are a lot of people looking for launch managers. There are more opportunities to be a launch manager
